metadata.summary: Schema-first GraphQL with SDL as source of truth, codegen-driven types, typed mutation payloads, per-request DataLoader for lists, and field-level authorization.

content.title: GraphQL API Design
content.summary: Schema-first GraphQL design where the SDL is the source of truth, codegen drives client and server types, and every mutation returns a typed MutationNamePayload with a userErrors array. Core rule: every list-returning resolver must use a DataLoader instantiated per-request; every field with sensitive data must have explicit field-level authorization.

why.p: Schema-first with codegen eliminates type drift between client and server. DataLoader prevents N+1 query explosions — the canonical GraphQL performance failure. Typed mutation payloads surface business errors structurally rather than as exceptions, enabling partial-success handling that REST cannot express. Query depth and cost limits prevent DoS from malicious nested queries.

list.item: Multiple clients (web + iOS + Android + partner) with divergent field needs over the same domain
Highly relational data where REST would force N+1 round-trips or sprawling ?expand= params
Schema-first development where the contract is the source of truth and codegen drives types
Subscriptions for live dashboards, chat, or collaborative editing
Federated services (Apollo Federation, GraphQL Hive) with one supergraph over many teams
Public cache-heavy CDN-fronted APIs — REST + HTTP cache headers wins
File upload or streaming binary payloads — multipart REST or signed URLs are simpler
Tiny CRUD app with a single client — schema overhead and N+1 traps are not worth it
Latency-sensitive RPC inside a cluster — gRPC is faster and stricter
When your team has zero GraphQL experience and the deadline is tight; learning curve hits hard at the resolver-and-dataloader stage

rule.statement: Treat schema.graphql (SDL) as the source of truth. Run codegen in CI and fail if generated files drift from the committed schema.
rule.rationale: SDL-first ensures the API contract is reviewed before any implementation. Committing generated files by hand allows them to diverge from the schema silently.

rule.statement: Every root type should implement Node (with id: ID!) and Timestamped (with createdAt, updatedAt). This enables consistent caching and relay compliance.
rule.rationale: Relay client caching is keyed by id. Without a consistent id field, clients cannot normalize the cache. Timestamps are universally needed for auditing and display.

rule.statement: Every mutation must return a typed payload: MutationNamePayload containing the result object and a userErrors: [Error!]! array. Never throw exceptions for business errors.
rule.rationale: HTTP always returns 200 for GraphQL. Exceptions become opaque errors[] entries. Typed payloads allow clients to handle business errors (validation, conflicts) structurally in the same type system as success responses.

section.name: Relay Connections

rule.statement: Use Relay cursor connections for every list field that could grow beyond 10 items. Never expose offset/limit pagination in the public schema.
rule.rationale: Offset pagination is unstable when items are inserted or deleted mid-page. Cursor pagination returns consistent results regardless of concurrent modifications. Relay clients depend on PageInfo for infinite scroll.

section.reference: Full reference schema with Connection types and mutation payloads

section.name: Best Practices
section.p: DataLoader per request, not per process; keys identity-equal to actual fetch key
Make every mutation return a typed MutationNamePayload with userErrors array
Persisted queries in production; reject unknown query hashes
Disable introspection in prod or gate behind admin auth
Field-level auth via policy layer, never sprinkle checks in resolvers

template.purpose: Full reference schema with Connection types
